html:
Act III

The drawing-room at ‚ÄúHighercoombe.‚Äù Facing the spectator are two large French windows, sheltered by a verandah, leading into the garden; on the right is a door opening into a small hall. The fireplace, with a large mirror above it, is on the left-hand side of the room, and higher up in the same wall are double doors recessed. The room is richly furnished, and everything betokens taste and luxury. The windows are open, and there is moonlight in the garden.

Lady Orreyed, a pretty, affected doll of a woman with a mincing voice and flaxen hair, is sitting on the ottoman, her head resting against the drum, and her eyes closed. Paula, looking pale, worn, and thoroughly unhappy, is sitting at a table. Both are in sumptuous dinner-gowns.
Lady Orreyed Opening her eyes. Well, I never! I dropped off! Feeling her hair. Just fancy! Where are the men?
Paula Icily. Outside, smoking.
A Servant enters with coffee, which he hands to Lady Orreyed. Sir George Orreyed comes in by the window. He is a man of about thirty-five, with a low forehead, a receding chin, a vacuous expression, and an ominous redness about the nose.
Lady Orreyed Taking coffee. Here‚Äôs Dodo.
Sir George I say, the flies under the verandah make you swear. The Servant hands coffee to Paula, who declines it, then to Sir George, who takes a cup. Hi! wait a bit! He looks at the tray searchingly, then puts back his cup. Never mind. Quietly to Lady Orreyed. I say, they‚Äôre dooced sparin‚Äô with their liqueur, ain‚Äôt they?
The Servant goes out at window.
Paula To Sir George. Won‚Äôt you take coffee, George?
Sir George No, thanks. It‚Äôs gettin‚Äô near time for a whisky and potass. Approaching Paula, regarding Lady Orreyed admiringly. I say, Birdie looks rippin‚Äô tonight, don‚Äôt she?
Paula Your wife?
Sir George Yaas‚ÅÝ‚ÄîBirdie.
Paula Rippin‚Äô?
Sir George Yaas.
Paula Quite‚ÅÝ‚Äîquite rippin‚Äô.
He moves round to the settee. Paula watches him with distaste, then rises and walks away. Sir George falls asleep on the settee.
Lady Orreyed Paula love, I fancied you and Aubrey were a little more friendly at dinner. You haven‚Äôt made it up, have you?
Paula We? Oh, no. We speak before others, that‚Äôs all.
Lady Orreyed And how long do you intend to carry on this game, dear?
Paula Turning away impatiently. I really can‚Äôt tell you.
Lady Orreyed Sit down, old girl; don‚Äôt be so fidgety. Paula sits on the upper seat of the ottoman with her back to Lady Orreyed. Of course, it‚Äôs my duty, as an old friend, to give you a good talking-to‚ÅÝ‚ÄîPaula glares at her suddenly and fiercely‚ÅÝ‚Äîbut really I‚Äôve found one gets so many smacks in the face through interfering in matrimonial squabbles that I‚Äôve determined to drop it.
Paula I think you‚Äôre wise.
Lady Orreyed However, I must say that I do wish you‚Äôd look at marriage in a more solemn light‚ÅÝ‚Äîjust as I do, in fact. It is such a beautiful thing‚ÅÝ‚Äîmarriage, and if people in our position don‚Äôt respect it, and set a good example by living happily with their husbands, what can you expect from the middle classes? When did this sad state of affairs between you and Aubrey actually begin?
Paula Actually, a fortnight and three days ago; I haven‚Äôt calculated the minutes.
Lady Orreyed A day or two before Dodo and I turned up‚ÅÝ‚Äîarrived.
Paula Yes. One always remembers one thing by another; we left off speaking to each other the morning I wrote asking you to visit us.
Lady Orreyed Lucky for you I was able to pop down, wasn‚Äôt it, dear?
Paula Glaring at her again. Most fortunate.
Lady Orreyed A serious split with your husband without a pal on the premises‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI should say, without a friend in the house‚ÅÝ‚Äîwould be most unpleasant.
Paula Turning to her abruptly. This place must be horribly doleful for you and George just now. At least you ought to consider him before me. Why don‚Äôt you leave me to my difficulties?
Lady Orreyed Oh, we‚Äôre quite comfortable, dear, thank you‚ÅÝ‚Äîboth of us. George and me are so wrapped up in each other, it doesn‚Äôt matter where we are. I don‚Äôt want to crow over you, old girl, but I‚Äôve got a perfect husband.
Sir George is now fast asleep, his head thrown back and his mouth open, looking hideous.
Paula Glancing at Sir George. So you‚Äôve given me to understand.
Lady Orreyed Not that we don‚Äôt have our little differences. Why, we fell out only this very morning. You remember the diamond and ruby tiara Charley Prestwick gave poor dear Connie Tirlemont years ago, don‚Äôt you?
Paula No, I do not.
Lady Orreyed No? Well, it‚Äôs in the market. Benjamin of Piccadilly has got it in his shopwindow, and I‚Äôve set my heart on it.
Paula You consider it quite necessary?
Lady Orreyed Yes, because what I say to Dodo is this‚ÅÝ‚Äîa lady of my station must smother herself with hair ornaments. It‚Äôs different with you, love‚ÅÝ‚Äîpeople don‚Äôt look for so much blaze from you, but I‚Äôve got rank to keep up; haven‚Äôt I?
Paula Yes.
Lady Orreyed Well, that was the cause of the little set-to between I and Dodo this morning. He broke two chairs, he was in such a rage. I forgot, they‚Äôre your chairs; do you mind?
Paula No.
Lady Orreyed You know, poor Dodo can‚Äôt lose his temper without smashing something; if it isn‚Äôt a chair, it‚Äôs a mirror; if it isn‚Äôt that, it‚Äôs china‚ÅÝ‚Äîa bit of Dresden for choice. Dear old pet! he loves a bit of Dresden when he‚Äôs furious. He doesn‚Äôt really throw things at me, dear; he simply lifts them up and drops them, like a gentleman. I expect our room upstairs will look rather wrecky before I get that tiara.
Paula Excuse the suggestion, perhaps your husband can‚Äôt afford it.
Lady Orreyed Oh, how dreadfully changed you are, Paula! Dodo can always mortgage something, or borrow of his ma. What is coming to you!
Paula Ah!
She sits at the piano and touches the keys.
Lady Orreyed Oh, yes, do play! That‚Äôs the one thing I envy you for.
Paula What shall I play?
Lady Orreyed What was that heavenly piece you gave us last night, dear?
Paula A bit of Schubert. Would you like to hear it again?
Lady Orreyed You don‚Äôt know any comic songs, do you?
Paula I‚Äôm afraid not.
Lady Orreyed I leave it to you, then.
Paula plays. Aubrey and Cayley Drummle appear outside the window; they look into the room.
Aubrey To Drummle. You can see her face in that mirror. Poor girl, how ill and wretched she looks.
Drummle When are the Orreyeds going?
Aubrey Heaven knows!
Entering the room.
Drummle But you‚Äôre entertaining them; what‚Äôs it to do with Heaven?
Following Aubrey.
Aubrey Do you know, Cayley, that even the Orreyeds serve a useful purpose? My wife actually speaks to me before our guests‚ÅÝ‚Äîthink of that! I‚Äôve come to rejoice at the presence of the Orreyeds!
Drummle I daresay; we‚Äôre taught that beetles are sent for a benign end.
Aubrey Cayley, talk to Paula again tonight.
Drummle Certainly, if I get the chance.
Aubrey Let‚Äôs contrive it. George is asleep; perhaps I can get that doll out of the way. As they advance into the room, Paula abruptly ceases playing and finds interest in a volume of music. Sir George is now nodding and snoring apoplectically. Lady Orreyed, whenever you feel inclined for a game of billiards I‚Äôm at your service.
Lady Orreyed Jumping up. Charmed, I‚Äôm sure! I really thought you‚Äôd forgotten poor little me. Oh, look at Dodo!
Aubrey No, no, don‚Äôt wake him; he‚Äôs tired.
Lady Orreyed I must, he looks so plain. Rousing Sir George. Dodo! Dodo!
Sir George Stupidly. ‚ÄôUllo!
Lady Orreyed Dodo, dear, you were snoring.
Sir George Oh, I say, you could ‚Äôa told me that by-and-by.
Aubrey You want a cigar, George; come into the billiard room. Giving his arm to Lady Orreyed. Cayley, bring Paula.
Aubrey and Lady Orreyed go out.
Sir George Rising. Hey, what! billiard room! Looking at his watch. How goes the‚ÅÝ‚Äî? Phew! ‚ÄôUllo, ‚ÄôUllo! Whisky and potass!
He goes rapidly after Aubrey and Lady Orreyed. Paula resumes playing.
Paula After a pause. Don‚Äôt moon about after me, Cayley; follow the others.
Drummle Thanks, by-and-by. Sitting. That‚Äôs pretty.
Paula After another pause, still playing. I wish you wouldn‚Äôt stare so.
Drummle Was I staring? I‚Äôm sorry. She plays a little longer, then stops suddenly, rises, and goes to the window, where she stands looking out. Drummle moves from the ottoman to the settee. A lovely night.
Paula Startled. Oh! Without turning to him. Why do you hop about like a monkey?
Drummle Hot rooms play the deuce with the nerves. Now, it would have done you good to have walked in the garden with us after dinner and made merry. Why didn‚Äôt you?
Paula You know why.
Drummle Ah, you‚Äôre thinking of the‚ÅÝ‚Äîdifference between you and Aubrey?
Paula Yes, I am thinking of it.
Drummle Well, so am I. How long‚ÅÝ‚Äî?
Paula Getting on for three weeks.
Drummle Bless me, it must be! And this would have been such a night to have healed it! Moonlight, the stars, the scent of flowers; and yet enough darkness to enable a kind woman to rest her hand for an instant on the arm of a good fellow who loves her. Ah, ha! it‚Äôs a wonderful power, dear Mrs.¬ÝAubrey, the power of an offended woman! Only realise it! Just that one touch‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe mere tips of her fingers‚ÅÝ‚Äîand, for herself and another, she changes the colour of the whole world!
Paula Turning to him, calmly. Cayley, my dear man, you talk exactly like a very romantic old lady.
She leaves the window and sits playing with the knickknacks on the table.
Drummle To himself. H‚Äôm, that hasn‚Äôt done it! Well‚ÅÝ‚Äîha, ha!‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI accept the suggestion. An old woman, eh?
Paula Oh, I didn‚Äôt intend‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Drummle But why not? I‚Äôve every qualification‚ÅÝ‚Äîwell, almost. And I confess it would have given this withered bosom a throb of grandmotherly satisfaction if I could have seen you and Aubrey at peace before I take my leave tomorrow.
Paula Tomorrow, Cayley!
Drummle I must.
Paula Oh, this house is becoming unendurable.
Drummle You‚Äôre very kind. But you‚Äôve got the Orreyeds.
Paula Fiercely. The Orreyeds! I‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI hate the Orreyeds! I lie awake at night, hating them!
Drummle Pardon me, I‚Äôve understood that their visit is, in some degree, owing to‚ÅÝ‚Äîhem!‚ÅÝ‚Äîyour suggestion.
Paula Heavens! that doesn‚Äôt make me like them better. Somehow or another, I‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI‚Äôve outgrown these people. This woman‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI used to think her ‚Äújolly!‚Äù‚ÅÝ‚Äîsickens me. I can‚Äôt breathe when she‚Äôs near me: the whiff of her handkerchief turns me faint! And she patronises me by the hour, until I‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI feel my nails growing longer with every word she speaks!
Drummle My dear lady, why on earth don‚Äôt you say all this to Aubrey?
Paula Oh, I‚Äôve been such an utter fool, Cayley!
Drummle Soothingly. Well, well, mention it to Aubrey!
Paula No, no, you don‚Äôt understand. What do you think I‚Äôve done?
Drummle Done! What, since you invited the Orreyeds?
Paula Yes; I must tell you‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Drummle Perhaps you‚Äôd better not.
Paula Look here. I‚Äôve intercepted some letters from Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon and Ellean to‚ÅÝ‚Äîhim. Producing three unopened letters from the bodice of her dress. There are the accursed things! From Paris‚ÅÝ‚Äîtwo from the Cortelyon woman, the other from Ellean!
Drummle But why‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhy?
Paula I don‚Äôt know. Yes, I do! I saw letters coming from Ellean to her father; not a line to me‚ÅÝ‚Äînot a line. And one morning it happened I was downstairs before he was, and I spied this one lying with his heap on the breakfast-table, and I slipped it into my pocket‚ÅÝ‚Äîout of malice, Cayley, pure devilry! And a day or two afterwards I met Elwes the postman at the Lodge, and took the letters from him, and found these others amongst ‚Äôem. I felt simply fiendish when I saw them‚ÅÝ‚Äîfiendish! Returning the letters to her bodice. And now I carry them about with me, and they‚Äôre scorching me like a mustard plaster!
Drummle Oh, this accounts for Aubrey not hearing from Paris lately!
Paula That‚Äôs an ingenious conclusion to arrive at! Of course it does! With an hysterical laugh. Ha, ha!
Drummle Well, well! Laughing. Ha, ha, ha!
Paula Turning upon him. I suppose it is amusing!
Drummle I beg pardon.
Paula Heaven knows I‚Äôve little enough to brag about! I‚Äôm a bad lot, but not in mean tricks of this sort. In all my life this is the most caddish thing I‚Äôve done. How am I to get rid of these letters‚ÅÝ‚Äîthat‚Äôs what I want to know? How am I to get rid of them?
Drummle If I were you I should take Aubrey aside and put them into his hands as soon as possible.
Paula What! and tell him to his face that I‚ÅÝ‚Äî! No, thank you. I suppose you wouldn‚Äôt like to‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Drummle No, no; I won‚Äôt touch ‚Äôem!
Paula And you call yourself my friend?
Drummle Good-humouredly. No, I don‚Äôt!
Paula Perhaps I‚Äôll tie them together and give them to his man in the morning.
Drummle That won‚Äôt avoid an explanation.
Paula Recklessly. Oh, then he must miss them‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Drummle And trace them.
Paula Throwing herself upon the ottoman. I don‚Äôt care!
Drummle I know you don‚Äôt; but let me send him to you now, may I?
Paula Now! What do you think a woman‚Äôs made of? I couldn‚Äôt stand it, Cayley. I haven‚Äôt slept for nights; and last night there was thunder, too! I believe I‚Äôve got the horrors.
Drummle Taking the little hand-mirror from the table. You‚Äôll sleep well enough when you deliver those letters. Come, come, Mrs.¬ÝAubrey‚ÅÝ‚Äîa good night‚Äôs rest! Holding the mirror before her face. It‚Äôs quite time.
She looks at herself for a moment, then snatches the mirror from him.
Paula You brute, Cayley, to show me that!
Drummle Then‚ÅÝ‚Äîmay I? Be guided by a fr‚ÅÝ‚Äîa poor old woman! May I?
Paula You‚Äôll kill me, amongst you!
Drummle What do you say?
Paula After a pause. Very well. He nods his head and goes out rapidly. She looks after him for a moment, and calls ‚ÄúCayley! Cayley!‚Äù Then she again produces the letters, deliberately, one by one, fingering them with aversion. Suddenly she starts, turning her head towards the door. Ah!
Aubrey enters quickly.
Aubrey Paula!
Paula Handing him the letters, her face averted. There! He examines the letters, puzzled, and looks at her inquiringly. They are many days old. I stole them, I suppose to make you anxious and unhappy.
He looks at the letters again, then lays them aside on the table.
Aubrey Gently. Paula, dear, it doesn‚Äôt matter.
Paula After a short pause. Why‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhy do you take it like this?
Aubrey What did you expect?
Paula Oh, but I suppose silent reproaches are really the severest. And then, naturally, you are itching to open your letters.
She crosses the room as if to go.
Aubrey Paula! She pauses. Surely, surely it‚Äôs all over now?
Paula All over! Mockingly. Has my stepdaughter returned then? When did she arrive? I haven‚Äôt heard of it!
Aubrey You can be very cruel.
Paula That word‚Äôs always on a man‚Äôs lips; he uses it if his soup‚Äôs cold. With another movement as if to go. Need we‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Aubrey I know I‚Äôve wounded you, Paula. But isn‚Äôt there any way out of this?
Paula When does Ellean return? Tomorrow? Next week?
Aubrey Wearily. Oh! Why should we grudge Ellean the little pleasure she is likely to find in Paris and in London.
Paula I grudge her nothing, if that‚Äôs a hit at me. But with that woman‚ÅÝ‚Äî!
Aubrey It must be that woman or another. You know that at present we are unable to give Ellean the opportunity of‚ÅÝ‚Äîof‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Paula Of mixing with respectable people.
Aubrey The opportunity of gaining friends, experience, ordinary knowledge of the world. If you are interested in Ellean, can‚Äôt you see how useful Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon‚Äôs good offices are?
Paula May I put one question? At the end of the London season, when Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon has done with Ellean, is it quite understood that the girl comes back to us? Aubrey is silent. Is it? Is it?
Aubrey Let us wait till the end of the season‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Paula Oh! I knew it. You‚Äôre only fooling me; you put me off with any trash. I believe you‚Äôve sent Ellean away, not for the reasons you give, but because you don‚Äôt consider me a decent companion for her, because you‚Äôre afraid she might get a little of her innocence rubbed off in my company? Come, isn‚Äôt that the truth? Be honest! Isn‚Äôt that it?
Aubrey Yes.
There is a moment‚Äôs silence on both sides.
Paula With uplifted hands as if to strike him. Oh!
Aubrey Taking her by the wrists. Sit down. Sit down. He puts her into a chair; she shakes herself free with a cry. Now listen to me. Fond as you are, Paula, of harking back to your past, there‚Äôs one chapter of it you always let alone. I‚Äôve never asked you to speak of it; you‚Äôve never offered to speak of it. I mean the chapter that relates to the time when you were‚ÅÝ‚Äîlike Ellean. She attempts to rise; he restrains her. No, no.
Paula I don‚Äôt choose to talk about that time. I won‚Äôt satisfy your curiosity.
Aubrey My dear Paula, I have no curiosity‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI know what you were at Ellean‚Äôs age. I‚Äôll tell you. You hadn‚Äôt a thought that wasn‚Äôt a wholesome one, you hadn‚Äôt an impulse that didn‚Äôt tend towards good, you never harboured a notion you couldn‚Äôt have gossiped about to a parcel of children. She makes another effort to rise: he lays his hand lightly on her shoulder. And this was a very few years back‚ÅÝ‚Äîthere are days now when you look like a schoolgirl‚ÅÝ‚Äîbut think of the difference between the two Paulas. You‚Äôll have to think hard, because after a cruel life one‚Äôs perceptions grow a thick skin. But, for God‚Äôs sake, do think till you get these two images clearly in your mind, and then ask yourself what sort of a friend such a woman as you are today would have been for the girl of seven or eight years ago.
Paula Rising. How dare you? I could be almost as good a friend to Ellean as her own mother would have been had she lived. I know what you mean. How dare you?
Aubrey You say that; very likely you believe it. But you‚Äôre blind, Paula; you‚Äôre blind. You! Every belief that a young, pure-minded girl holds sacred‚ÅÝ‚Äîthat you once held sacred‚ÅÝ‚Äîyou now make a target for a jest, a sneer, a paltry cynicism. I tell you, you‚Äôre not mistress any longer of your thoughts or your tongue. Why, how often, sitting between you and Ellean, have I seen her cheeks turn scarlet as you‚Äôve rattled off some tale that belongs by right to the club or the smoking-room! Have you noticed the blush? If you have, has the cause of it ever struck you? And this is the girl you say you love, I admit that you do love, whose love you expect in return! Oh, Paula, I make the best, the only, excuse for you when I tell you you‚Äôre blind!
Paula Ellean‚ÅÝ‚ÄîEllean blushes easily.
Aubrey You blushed as easily a few years ago.
Paula After a short pause. Well! Have you finished your sermon?
Aubrey With a gesture of despair. Oh, Paula!
Going up to the window and standing with his back to the room.
Paula To herself. A few‚ÅÝ‚Äîyears ago! She walks slowly towards the door, then suddenly drops upon the ottoman in a paroxysm of weeping. O God! A few years ago!
Aubrey Going to her. Paula!
Paula Sobbing. Oh, don‚Äôt touch me!
Aubrey Paula!
Paula Oh, go away from me! He goes back a few steps, and after a little while she becomes calmer and rises unsteadily; then in an altered tone. Look here‚ÅÝ‚Äî! He advances a step; she checks him with a quick gesture. Look here! Get rid of these people‚ÅÝ‚ÄîMabel and her husband‚ÅÝ‚Äîas soon as possible! I‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI‚Äôve done with them!
Aubrey In a whisper. Paula!
Paula And then‚ÅÝ‚Äîthen‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhen the time comes for Ellean to leave Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon, give me‚ÅÝ‚Äîgive me another chance! He advances again, but she shrinks away. No, no!
She goes out by the door on the right. He sinks on to the settee, covering his eyes with his hands. There is a brief silence, then a Servant enters.
Servant Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon, sir, with Miss Ellean.
Aubrey rises to meet Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon, who enters, followed by Ellean, both being in travelling dresses. The Servant withdraws.
Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon Shaking hands with Aubrey. Oh, my dear Aubrey!
Aubrey Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon! Kissing Ellean. Ellean dear!
Ellean Papa, is all well at home?
Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon We‚Äôre shockingly anxious.
Aubrey Yes, yes, all‚Äôs well. This is quite unexpected. To Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon. You‚Äôve found Paris insufferably hot?
Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon Insufferably hot! Paris is pleasant enough. We‚Äôve had no letter from you!
Aubrey I wrote to Ellean a week ago.
Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon Without alluding to the subject I had written to you upon.
Aubrey Thinking. Ah, of course‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon And since then we‚Äôve both written and you‚Äôve been absolutely silent. Oh, it‚Äôs too bad!
Aubrey Picking up the letters from the table. It isn‚Äôt altogether my fault. Here are the letters‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Ellean Papa!
Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon They‚Äôre unopened.
Aubrey An accident delayed their reaching me till this evening. I‚Äôm afraid this has upset you very much.
Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon Upset me!
Ellean In an undertone to Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon. Never mind. Not now, dear‚ÅÝ‚Äînot tonight.
Aubrey Eh?
Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon To Ellean aloud. Child, run away and take your things off. She doesn‚Äôt look as if she‚Äôd journeyed from Paris today.
Aubrey I‚Äôve never seen her with such a colour.
Taking Ellean‚Äôs hands.
Ellean To Aubrey, in a faint voice. Papa, Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon has been so very, very kind to me, but I‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI have come home.
She goes out.
Aubrey Come home! To Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon. Ellean returns to us, then?
Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon That‚Äôs the very point I put to you in my letters, and you oblige me to travel from Paris to Willowmere on a warm day to settle it. I think perhaps it‚Äôs right that Ellean should be with you just now, although I‚ÅÝ‚ÄîMy dear friend, circumstances are a little altered.
Aubrey Alice, you‚Äôre in some trouble.
Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon Well‚ÅÝ‚Äîyes, I am in trouble. You remember pretty little Mrs.¬ÝBrereton who was once Caroline Ardale?
Aubrey Quite well.
Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon She‚Äôs a widow now, poor thing. She has the entresol of the house where we‚Äôve been lodging in the Avenue de Friedland. Caroline‚Äôs a dear chum of mine; she formed a great liking for Ellean.
Aubrey I‚Äôm very glad.
Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon Yes, it‚Äôs nice for her to meet her mother‚Äôs friends. Er‚ÅÝ‚Äîthat young Hugh Ardale the papers were full of some time ago‚ÅÝ‚Äîhe‚Äôs Caroline Brereton‚Äôs brother, you know.
Aubrey No, I didn‚Äôt know. What did he do? I forget.
Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon Checked one of those horrid mutinies at some faraway station in India, marched down with a handful of his men and a few faithful natives, and held the place until he was relieved. They gave him his company and a V.C. for it.
Aubrey And he‚Äôs Mrs.¬ÝBrereton‚Äôs brother?
Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon Yes. He‚Äôs with his sister‚ÅÝ‚Äîwas, rather‚ÅÝ‚Äîin Paris. He‚Äôs home‚ÅÝ‚Äîinvalided. Good gracious, Aubrey, why don‚Äôt you help me out? Can‚Äôt you guess what has occurred?
Aubrey Alice!
Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon Young Ardale‚ÅÝ‚ÄîEllean!
Aubrey An attachment?
Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon Yes, Aubrey. After a little pause. Well, I suppose I‚Äôve got myself into sad disgrace. But really I didn‚Äôt foresee anything of this kind. A serious, reserved child like Ellean, and a boyish, high-spirited soldier‚ÅÝ‚Äîit never struck me as being likely. Aubrey paces to and fro thoughtfully. I did all I could directly Captain Ardale spoke‚ÅÝ‚Äîwrote to you at once. Why on earth don‚Äôt you receive your letters promptly, and when you do get them why can‚Äôt you open them? I endured the anxiety till last night, and then made up my mind‚ÅÝ‚Äîhome! Of course, it has worried me terribly. My head‚Äôs bursting. Are there any salts about? Aubrey fetches a bottle from the cabinet and hands it to her. We‚Äôve had one of those hateful smooth crossings that won‚Äôt let you be properly indisposed.
Aubrey My dear Alice, I assure you I‚Äôve no thought of blaming you.
Mrs.¬ÝCortelyon That statement always precedes a quarrel.
Aubrey I don‚Äôt know whether this is the worst or the best luck. How will my wife regard it? Is Captain Ardale a good fellow?
